name: Lucky Bamboo
description: Lucky Bamboo (Dracaena sanderiana) is a popular decorative plant often sold in water or soil arrangements. Despite its name, it is not a true bamboo — it is a Dracaena species and shares the same toxicity profile as other Dracaena plants. It contains steroidal saponins that irritate the gastrointestinal tract and produce cat-specific neurological effects.
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: steroidal saponins (consistent with Dracaena genus)
Mechanism: saponins disrupt cell membrane integrity causing GI irritation; neurological effects noted in cats specifically
Symptoms: vomiting (sometimes blood-tinged), drooling, loss of appetite, weakness, dilated pupils (mydriasis)
Dilated pupils are a notable cat-specific sign of Dracaena/Lucky Bamboo toxicity
Water containing Lucky Bamboo may also carry some toxins — prevent cats from drinking from the vase

symptoms.name: Drooling
symptoms.onset: Rapid / immediate upon chewing (specific time not provided in sources).
symptoms.notes: This is an initial reaction to the irritation of the mouth and gastrointestinal tract caused by the plant's saponins13. 2. Vomiting

symptoms.name: Vomiting (which can occasionally contain blood)
symptoms.notes: Vomiting is one of the most common signs of Dracaena ingestion1.... If vomiting becomes severe or persistent, the cat must be monitored closely for dehydration1. 3. Diarrhea

symptoms.name: Diarrhea
symptoms.onset: Variable (typically occurs as the toxins move through the digestive tract).
symptoms.notes: Veterinary sources note that the diarrhea can be severe, reflecting significant gastrointestinal irritation from the plant's compounds12. 4. Abdominal Pain and Loss of Appetite

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain and inappetence (anorexia)
symptoms.notes: Due to the severe stomach upset and irritation, cats will often experience stomach discomfort and a subsequent refusal to eat1.... 5. Dilated Pupils

symptoms.name: Dilated pupils (mydriasis)
symptoms.notes: The presence of noticeably wide or dilated pupils is a unique clinical sign specifically highlighted in cats that have ingested Dracaena species1.... 6. Depression, Weakness, and Incoordination

symptoms.name: Depression (lethargy), generalized weakness, and incoordination (ataxia)
symptoms.onset: Delayed (usually follows the initial GI distress).
symptoms.notes: While small ingestions are generally self-limiting, these systemic and neurological symptoms are known to manifest if a cat consumes a large amount of the plant or becomes severely weakened from refusing to eat
